title: Lisa Gets An A

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer getting into some kind of accident during the opening credits
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation followed by screaming
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase that's become culturally iconic - perfect character integration and economy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *screaming*

jokes.text: A... Men-nonite minister will be giving a guest sermon next Sunday
jokes.setup: Traditional church service ending with 'Amen'
jokes.punchline: Stretching it into an announcement about a Mennonite minister
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that transforms religious routine into comedy through pun structure

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Amen... and speaking of which, we have a visiting Mennonite minister in the congregation today.

jokes.text: Don't make me come up there / Let me outta here. The guy never stops talking
jokes.setup: Reverend threatening congregation members
jokes.punchline: Moe complaining about the minister talking too much right after the threat
jokes.explanation: Nice ironic timing with Moe's complaint immediately following Lovejoy's authority assertion

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Now, I'm warning you all - if you don't increase your donations, there will be consequences!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Oh, here we go again. This guy talks more than my ex-wife. And she never shut up about my gambling problem.

jokes.text: Mom, can we go Catholic so we can get Communion wafers and booze?
jokes.setup: Bart being hungry after church
jokes.punchline: Suggesting converting religions for the free food and wine
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart logic - practical and irreverent approach to religion that only he would voice

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, I'm starving. Church really works up an appetite.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we could try a different religion. I hear the Jews get a free meal at the end of their service.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah? What about the Catholics?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just some stale crackers and cheap wine.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, we should convert religions every week. Free food and wine!

jokes.text: No one's going Catholic. Three children is enough, thank you
jokes.setup: Bart asking to convert to Catholicism
jokes.punchline: Marge rejecting it based on Catholic stance against birth control
jokes.explanation: Sharp adult humor disguised as innocent response - references Catholic birth control doctrine

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, can I convert to Catholicism?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Absolutely not! Do you know what the Catholics believe about birth control?

jokes.text: He zinged you, Marge
jokes.setup: Driver calls Homer an idiot
jokes.punchline: Homer treating the insult as a clever comeback worth acknowledging
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious nature - completely missing that he was the target of the insult

dialogue.speaker: Driver
dialogue.line: You're an idiot!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, the jerk store called, and they're all out of you!

jokes.text: I feel guilty coming here every Sunday and never actually buying anything / Why? We're following the rules. If it has a toothpick in it, it's free
jokes.setup: Marge expressing guilt about their behavior at what's clearly a grocery store
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted logic justifying their mooching based on sample rules
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer-Marge dynamic: her conscience vs. his opportunistic rule-bending

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I feel terrible about taking all these free samples without buying anything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, Marge, Marge. The sign says 'Free Samples.' It doesn't say 'Free Samples, Then You Have to Buy Something.' If they wanted us to buy stuff, they'd make a sign that says 'Samples: $5 Each.' But it doesn't! So technically, we're following the rules.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's not how it works, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Denial is just a river in Egypt, Marge.

jokes.text: Soy substitute. 'Whizless' cheese. Oven-roasted cud. It's packed in its own drool
jokes.setup: Homer exploring health food section
jokes.punchline: Increasingly disgusting fake food products with revolting descriptions
jokes.explanation: Escalating grossness with creative fake product names, building to disgusting climax

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, health food! Finally, a way to eat without getting fat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hmm, what's this? 'Tofu Dogs - All the taste of a real hot dog with none of the flavor.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And here's 'Kelp Chips - The ocean's gift wrapped in seaweed and despair.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, 'Wheat Germ Brownies - For people who hate chocolate almost as much as they hate themselves.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And my personal favorite: 'Nutritional Yeast Spread - Yellow, flavorless, and slightly sentient.'

jokes.text: Oven-roasted cud. It's packed in its own drool.
jokes.setup: Reading disgusting health food products
jokes.punchline: Increasingly gross product description
jokes.explanation: Escalating grossness works well, though could be from any character

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Mmm, let's see what we have here... 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab.' Well, that doesn't sound too bad.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my. 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab Meal.' Hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab Meal Substitute.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab Meal Substitute Food Product.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab Meal Substitute Food Product Made From Seaweed.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: 'Krusty Brand Imitation Crab Meal Substitute Food Product Made From Seaweed With Artificial and Natural Flavors and Coloring and BHA and BHT and TBHQ.'

jokes.text: Those suck! I hate this store! I hate you too! I wanna live with one of my dads
jokes.setup: Child having tantrum about cereal choice
jokes.punchline: Escalating to family dysfunction and custody arrangements
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about modern family dysfunction hidden in child's grocery store tantrum

dialogue.speaker: Gavin
dialogue.line: I want the Frosted Flakes!

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: We have Cheerios.

dialogue.speaker: Gavin
dialogue.line: I WANT FROSTED FLAKES! YOU'RE RUINING MY LIFE!

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: It's just cereal, Gavin.

dialogue.speaker: Gavin
dialogue.line: YOU DON'T LOVE ME! I'm calling Dad's lawyer!

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: Your father lives here too.

dialogue.speaker: Gavin
dialogue.line: Then I'm emancipating myself AND the dog!

jokes.text: So, you say this product is known as 'fudge'? / Yes. Just like it was last week / If you're gonna get snippy, I'll take my business elsewhere
jokes.setup: Customer asking obvious question about fudge for second time
jokes.punchline: Getting offended when called out and threatening to leave over employee's exasperation
jokes.explanation: Generic difficult customer bit - functional but not particularly creative

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Is this fudge made with real fudge?

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Is this fudge made with real fudge?

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: Yes! We just went over this!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Well, I don't appreciate your tone. I'm taking my business elsewhere.

jokes.text: 'Jerry Garcia'? 'Honey Bono'? 'Desmond Tutti-frutti'?
jokes.setup: Homer looking at ice cream flavors
jokes.punchline: Celebrity names twisted into ice cream flavors with puns
jokes.explanation: Standard celebrity name puns for ice cream flavors - competent but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... Vanilla Ice... Mint Condition... Cookie Dough Biden... Rocky Road to Perdition... Cone Zoolander... Ben & Jerry Garcia... Macha Farka... Cookie Monster Munch... Sugar Free Willy...

jokes.text: 'Candy Warhol.' 'Xavier Nougat.' Naw. Nothin' made of dead guys
jokes.setup: More celebrity ice cream flavors being read
jokes.punchline: Homer's practical concern about eating flavors named after dead celebrities
jokes.explanation: Unexpected dark logic from Homer - treating celebrity death as ingredient concern

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, what about the dead celebrities? We got Ben & Jerry's, but what about Marilyn Monroe ice cream?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that would be disrespectful.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not disrespecting her, I'm eating her!

jokes.text: Lisa getting trapped in freezer and shivering
jokes.setup: Homer sending Lisa to look in back of freezer
jokes.punchline: She gets stuck and is obviously freezing cold
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy setup that serves plot purpose more than pure laughs

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, go look in the back of the freezer for me.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm stuck! It's freezing back here!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just a little longer, sweetie.

jokes.text: 'Sherbert Hoover'? We've gotta get you home to a warm blanket and a cold compress
jokes.setup: Lisa is clearly sick from the freezer incident
jokes.punchline: Homer still focused on ice cream flavors while acknowledging her illness
jokes.explanation: Homer's priorities showing - even concern for Lisa includes ice cream flavor observation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're sick from the freezer incident, but look - they have mint chip!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm freezing and my lips are turning blue.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... mint chip...

jokes.text: Eight dollars a pound times, say, oh, five pounds is, um- Let's see. How many pounds in a gallon? Oh, I can't afford that
jokes.setup: Homer trying to calculate lobster cost
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong math converting pounds to gallons, then giving up
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer stupidity - confidently using wrong units then accepting defeat

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... a lobster costs $15 a pound, and I need 5 pounds... that's... carry the one... 60 gallons!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh, math is hard. *walks away*

jokes.text: Eight dollars a pound times, say, oh, five pounds is, um- Let's see. How many pounds in a gallon?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to calculate lobster cost
jokes.punchline: Homer confusing weight and volume measurements
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer stupidity - fundamental confusion about basic measurements

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see, a lobster costs $20 a pound... I weigh 250 pounds... so I should cost $5,000!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well then how much would I cost if I was liquid?

jokes.text: I buy an eight-dollar lobster... fatten it into an 80-dollar lobster and eat the profits
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his 'financial genius' plan
jokes.punchline: Completely backwards understanding of profit - eating the product instead of selling it
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic failure - understanding concept of investment but missing the selling part

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've got a foolproof plan to make money. I bought a case of donuts wholesale, and I'm gonna eat them all and then sell the box!

jokes.text: Lobsters need saltwater. / I'm way ahead of you
jokes.setup: Lisa pointing out basic lobster care fact
jokes.punchline: Homer claiming to be ahead while clearly having no plan
jokes.explanation: Homer's false confidence - claiming preparedness when clearly unprepared

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you can't just keep a lobster in the freezer. They need to be in saltwater.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Relax, Lisa. I've got this all figured out. I'm three steps ahead of you.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What are the three steps?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... step one: don't listen to Lisa. Step two: something about salt. Step three: profit!

jokes.text: Dad, the fish! / Son, I'm still fine-tuning. Uh-oh. Perfect
jokes.setup: Homer clearly killing fish while 'fine-tuning' water
jokes.punchline: Declaring success right after obvious failure
jokes.explanation: Homer's delusional optimism in face of obvious disaster

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There we go. Perfect pH balance.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, all the fish are dead.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: I'm afraid I can't allow that. / Lisa! / Mom! No, wait. We can make a deal
jokes.setup: Marge trying to keep sick Lisa home from school
jokes.punchline: Lisa desperately trying to negotiate to stay sick rather than miss school
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - treating education like a compulsion she can't control

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're staying home from school today. You're sick.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Mom, I can't miss school! I have a test in every class, my debate team is competing, and I promised to tutor Ralphie!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Honey, you have a 103-degree fever. You need rest.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What if I just sit in the back of the classroom and don't participate? No one will even know I'm there. I'll be like a ghost. A silent, scholarly ghost.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, no.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'll wear a mask! Two masks! I'll basically be in quarantine while still attending school. It's the perfect solution.

jokes.text: You don't have anything I want
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to negotiate with Marge about staying home sick
jokes.punchline: Coldly dismissing her own mother's negotiating power
jokes.explanation: Brutal honesty from Lisa treating family negotiation like business transaction

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, I'm too sick to go to school.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, you look fine to me.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have a temperature of 103 degrees.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, you're still going to school.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, please. I'm begging you.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. Now get your backpack.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, for someone who claims to care about my well-being, you're not a very effective negotiator.

jokes.text: Oh, you shouldn't be watching The Learning Channel. You need to take it easy. / But I'm hardly learning at all
jokes.setup: Marge telling sick Lisa to avoid educational content
jokes.punchline: Lisa's disappointment that she's not absorbing information while ill
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa obsession with learning - frustrated even about reduced comprehension when sick

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, honey, when you're sick, you should avoid educational content. Your brain needs rest.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Mom, all I'm doing is lying here. How am I supposed to learn anything?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's the point, sweetie. Just rest.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is... I'm wasting a perfectly good opportunity to be sick without getting any smarter?

jokes.text: Why don't you play with one of Bart's video games instead? Hugh Downs says they're the latest craze
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting video games as sick day activity
jokes.punchline: Getting her information about youth culture from elderly news anchor
jokes.explanation: Marge's generational disconnect - relying on Hugh Downs for video game trend updates

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why don't you play one of those video games? I hear they're all the rage with the kids.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: This just in: video games are popular with today's youth.

jokes.text: Foolish dingo. You must find and devour the seven crystal babies... or spend eternity trapped in deep didgeridoo!
jokes.setup: Australian-themed video game giving instructions
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous fake-Australian dialogue with nonsensical quest
jokes.explanation: Parody of video game dialogue with exaggerated Australian stereotypes and gaming clichés

dialogue.speaker: Video Game
dialogue.line: G'day mate! Now crikey, that's a big Sheila! You'll need to collect 99 cans of Foster's to win her heart. Watch out for the deadly drop bears and remember: always say 'no worries' after every sentence, no worries.

jokes.text: What? Nunchakus? Those aren't even Australian
jokes.setup: Lisa finding martial arts weapons in Australian-themed game
jokes.punchline: Logical critique of game's cultural inconsistency
jokes.explanation: Lisa applying critical thinking to video game logic - typical analytical approach

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Why does an Australian-themed game have martial arts weapons? That's culturally inconsistent — Australia doesn't have a significant martial arts tradition.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Who cares, Lisa? It's just a game.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I care! It perpetuates stereotypes and shows a lack of research into authentic cultural representation.

jokes.text: Yes! I am just four power wands away from an antigravity lozenge
jokes.setup: Lisa initially dismissing the video game
jokes.punchline: Suddenly deeply invested in collecting ridiculous game items
jokes.explanation: Lisa's intellectual resistance crumbling to video game addiction - character development through gaming

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Video games are a mindless waste of time that rot your brain and corrupt your values.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, whatever. Check out this rare golden mushroom I just found.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, how rare are we talking? Because I read that only 0.003% of players have ever obtained one.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Pretty rare. You want to help me find more?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I suppose I could assist you in a purely academic capacity to study the game's algorithmic drop rates. Hand me the controller. And don't touch anything—I need to optimize my farming route to maximize mythical artifact acquisition.

jokes.text: Is this my house? / No. You live in a different house
jokes.setup: Ralph asking obvious question about location
jokes.punchline: Lisa's patient but literal response to Ralph's confusion
jokes.explanation: Ralph's consistent stupidity met with Lisa's matter-of-fact correction

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Mommy, where do babies come from?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well Ralph, when a mommy and daddy love each other very much, they...

jokes.text: Okay. Together, we weigh 300 pounds. According to my driver's license, I weigh 140 pounds. That means that you weigh, oh- 160 pounds!
jokes.setup: Homer doing math about weight distribution
jokes.punchline: Using obviously false driver's license weight as baseline for calculation
jokes.explanation: Homer's self-deception about his weight leading to absurd lobster weight calculation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... if I weigh 239 pounds according to my driver's license...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That means I'm only 180 pounds overweight!

jokes.text: Oh! You're doin' great. No, no. Yes, that's it! Bite! Bite! Bite!
jokes.setup: Homer coaching Pinchy the lobster
jokes.punchline: Encouraging aggressive behavior in what should be a gentle pet
jokes.explanation: Homer's misguided parenting instincts applied to dangerous crustacean

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, Pinchy! Bite him! Bite him! Yeah, that's it! Bite his head off!

dialogue.speaker: Pet Store Owner
dialogue.line: Sir, that's a hermit crab.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, bite HIS head off!

jokes.text: How do I get that kind of credibility? / With eight years of scrupulous honesty. / Eh. It's not worth it
jokes.setup: Bart asking how Lisa gets trusted when sick
jokes.punchline: Immediately dismissing the effort required for honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - learning moral lesson then rejecting it for being too much work

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: How do you always get away with being sick?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm honest about my symptoms.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Honest? That sounds like a lot of work.

jokes.text: But I- I might infect the other kids. / That's a risk I'm willing to take
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to stay home to protect others
jokes.punchline: Marge callously dismissing other children's health
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about parental priorities - protecting own schedule over community health

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, I can't go to school. I might get sick and spread it to everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, Lisa, don't be silly. Those other children will be fine.

jokes.text: Game over, mate
jokes.setup: Lisa failing the test just like dying in the video game
jokes.punchline: Same Australian accent delivering failure message in both contexts
jokes.explanation: Clever callback connecting Lisa's academic failure to gaming failure with same voice

dialogue.speaker: Video Game
dialogue.line: You are dead.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: You are dead.

jokes.text: Mr. blank needs a blank in order to blank his blank. Oh, I am in deep blank
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to answer test questions without reading
jokes.punchline: Escalating desperation ending in self-aware profanity substitute
jokes.explanation: Escalating frustration with clever censored swearing that fits Lisa's character

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Okay, I can do this. I'll just use my natural instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A) The mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell. That's... definitely one of them.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: B) Photosynthesis happens when... plants do that thing with the sun?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: C) DNA stands for... Darn, Nothing's obvious!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: D) The water cycle is... wet... dry... wet again... Son of a—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Diddly-doodly!

jokes.text: Weren't you at Brown, Otto? / Yep. Almost got tenure too
jokes.setup: Discussion of Brown University as lesser institution
jokes.punchline: School bus driver claiming near-tenure at prestigious university
jokes.explanation: Unexpected character backstory that subverts expectations about Otto while continuing Brown joke

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Brown University? That's not a real school. It's what you tell people when you flunk out of a real college.

dialogue.speaker: Otto
dialogue.line: Hey man, I almost had tenure there.

jokes.text: Brown. Brown. Brown.
jokes.setup: Lisa's academic anxiety about university prospects
jokes.punchline: Repetitive panic about attending Brown instead of Harvard
jokes.explanation: Lisa's perfectionism causing breakdown over still-excellent university options

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh no, what if I only get into Brown? Brown! Not Harvard, not Yale, but Brown!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'll be stuck in Providence, Rhode Island. Providence! Do you know what's in Providence? I don't, and that's the problem!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My entire future is ruined. Ruined, I tell you. Brown University. It might as well be named Mediocre State.

jokes.text: It's a little something I whipped up in shop. Mostly latex
jokes.setup: Bart appearing to be in class during school hours
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's using fake body part made in shop class to skip
jokes.explanation: Bart's creative approach to rule-breaking using school resources for deception

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Mrs. Krabappel, I'm not feeling well. I think I need to go to the nurse's office.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Bart, you look fine to me. Sit down.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Mrs. Krabappel, look at my arm!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I made it in shop class!

jokes.text: Well, if it was me, I'd just take the zero / But that's not for everyone
jokes.setup: Lisa asking Bart for academic advice
jokes.punchline: Bart suggesting his low-effort approach then acknowledging it won't work for her
jokes.explanation: Bart showing unexpected self-awareness about his different academic standards

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, I need your advice. How do you manage to get through school with so little effort?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, it's simple, man. You just don't care about grades, charm your teachers, and maybe copy homework from a smart kid.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's terrible advice.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. That won't work for you anyway. You actually have standards.

jokes.text: Relax. There's nothing here you didn't see when dad boycotted pants
jokes.setup: Lisa hesitant about entering boys' bathroom
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous Homer incident normalizing the situation
jokes.explanation: Casual reference to absurd family history to comfort Lisa about bathroom entry

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't go in the boys' bathroom!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh come on, it's not that bad. Besides, remember when Dad got stuck in there for three days?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That doesn't make me feel better, Bart.

jokes.text: Hey, hey. These are study aids. They're for novelty purposes only. If a few bad apples use 'em for cheating, I can't be held responsible
jokes.setup: Lisa horrified at discovering test answers
jokes.punchline: Nelson's elaborate legal disclaimer for obvious cheating operation
jokes.explanation: Corporate-speak applied to elementary school cheating ring - sophisticated criminal thinking

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: You want the test answers? Sure, I got 'em. But first, you gotta sign this waiver stating that I'm not responsible if you get caught, if the answers are wrong, if you spontaneously combust, or if you suddenly develop an irrational fear of geometry. Also, by purchasing these answers, you agree that Nelson Muntz is not liable for any emotional distress caused by failing anyway. Read the fine print.

jokes.text: Good for you, lass. I got me a zero once, and my life turned out just fine. Ach! Whoa-ho! That's a nasty clog
jokes.setup: Willie encouraging Lisa's moral stance with life advice
jokes.punchline: Immediately proving his point wrong by dealing with toilet disaster
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline where Willie's life advice is immediately undermined by his current situation

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Ye see, Lisa, the moral high ground is always the right choice, no matter how difficult...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Thank you, Willie. That means a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Aye, well— Oh, sweet mother of— The toilet's overflowed again! Get me the dynamite!

jokes.text: Good news, my delicious friend. You're going to be a free-range lobster
jokes.setup: Homer addressing Pinchy with apparent good news
jokes.punchline: Using food industry marketing term while still planning to eat him
jokes.explanation: Homer's misunderstanding of humane animal treatment while maintaining predatory relationship

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Pinchy. I'm not going to eat you. You're going to be my pet!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I'm going to eat you. But first, you're going to be free-range!

jokes.text: I can't stay mad at such a helpless little mammal
jokes.setup: Homer showing affection for Pinchy after being pinched
jokes.punchline: Calling a crustacean a mammal while showing misplaced tenderness
jokes.explanation: Homer's biological ignorance combined with genuine affection for dangerous pet

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, Pinchy! You're my little friend! Yes you are! Yes you are!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna take you home, give you a nice bath, and in a few years, we'll have beautiful children together.

jokes.text: Oh, look at him cower. Just like the boy. I can't stay mad at such a helpless little mammal.
jokes.setup: Homer comparing lobster to Bart
jokes.punchline: Homer's tender moment with lobster after insulting Bart
jokes.explanation: Good Homer parenting comparison - showing more affection for lobster than son

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, this lobster is pretty cute. I'm gonna call him Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh wait, I have a son named Bart. Eh, forget it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, lobster, you're gonna have to die. But first, let me tell you something... I love you.

jokes.text: No, Ralph. That's an 'F.' I must have spilled some Kahlua
jokes.setup: Ralph thinking he got a B on his test
jokes.punchline: Teacher's alcohol causing grade confusion
jokes.explanation: Teacher drinking on job leading to grade mix-up - dark humor about education system

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I got a B!

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: No Ralph, that's an 8. You got an F.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: But the B is right there!

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: That's the number of drinks I had last night. I was grading papers.

jokes.text: No. That's Drambuie. Mmm!
jokes.setup: Previous alcohol spill explanation
jokes.punchline: Identifying different alcohol while tasting it during class
jokes.explanation: Escalating Miss Hoover's drinking problem with specific liqueur knowledge

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: Class, I've identified the alcohol by taste. It's a 1987 Château Lafite.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Wow, Miss Hoover, you're smart!

dialogue.speaker: Miss Hoover
dialogue.line: Well, I may have had a drinking problem in my past.

jokes.text: But, Marge, look at this little guy / [Growling, Hissing] / Looks like an ordinary- Ow! Son of a-
jokes.setup: Homer trying to show Pinchy's cuteness to Marge
jokes.punchline: Pinchy immediately attacking Marge mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Classic timing joke where cute pet demonstration goes immediately wrong

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at how cute he is, Marge!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose he is rather adora—

dialogue.speaker: Pinchy
dialogue.line: *attacks*

jokes.text: Pardon me for asking... but where the hell's my stupid lobster? / We're not eating Mr. Pinchy. He's part of the family now
jokes.setup: Family dinner without expected lobster main course
jokes.punchline: Homer's complete emotional reversal treating food as family member
jokes.explanation: Homer's attachment to intended meal reaching absurd family integration level

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Where's the lobster? I specifically said lobster!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we're having pot roast tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Pot roast?! That's not what I ordered! This is worse than that time I had to eat that sandwich with mayo on it!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, just try it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fine. I'll eat this... beautiful, wonderful, magnificent pot roast. Oh, pot roast, you complete me. You're the love of my life. Kids, say goodbye to your mother.

jokes.text: It just turned up in the course of my daily rummaging. By the way, I oiled the hinge on your diary
jokes.setup: Marge presenting Lisa's test paper
jokes.punchline: Casually admitting to searching Lisa's room and maintaining her diary
jokes.explanation: Marge's invasive parenting disguised as helpful maintenance work

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, honey, I found your test paper. I was looking through your room, and I found your diary too.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom! You read my diary?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I'm not worried. You're doing great in school.

jokes.text: Oh! I am sick of everyone being so proud of me! That's my girl.
jokes.setup: Lisa expressing frustration about undeserved praise
jokes.punchline: Bart's pride in Lisa's corruption
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment - Bart finally proud of Lisa when she acts dishonestly

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe people think I'm smart just because I got an A on that test. I didn't even study!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's my girl. Welcome to the dark side, Lisa.

jokes.text: You got a good grade without even reading the book. That's win-win. / Can't you see the difference between earning something honestly... and getting it by fraud? / Hmm. I suppose, maybe, if, uh- No. No, sorry. I thought I had it there for a sec
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining moral distinction between honest and dishonest achievement
jokes.punchline: Bart briefly considering ethics then giving up entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - momentarily grasping morality then abandoning it as too complicated

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The key distinction is between honest achievement and dishonest gain. One builds character while the other—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, yeah. So basically you're saying I should work hard and do the right thing?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nah.

jokes.text: Can't you see the difference between earning something honestly... and getting it by fraud? Hmm. I suppose, maybe, if, uh- No. No, sorry. I thought I had it there for a sec.
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining moral difference between honesty and fraud
jokes.punchline: Bart genuinely trying but failing to understand the concept
jokes.explanation: Excellent Bart characterization - genuinely trying to understand ethics but failing completely

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Honesty means telling the truth, even when it's difficult. Fraud is deliberately deceiving someone for personal gain. Do you understand the moral difference?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, I think so... So fraud is like when you tell the truth but people think you're lying?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, Bart, that's the opposite of what I just said.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Right, right. So honesty is when you lie... but nobody knows?

jokes.text: I'll give you the numerators free... but the denominators are gonna cost ya
jokes.setup: Nelson offering Lisa test answers for math
jokes.punchline: Treating fraction components as separate commercial products
jokes.explanation: Brilliant business model applied to math cheating - understanding value of partial information

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Hey Simpson, I got the answers to tomorrow's math test. You want 'em?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Nelson, I can't cheat. That's wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Look, I'm not asking you to cheat. I'm offering you a consumer choice. The numerator comes from Acme, the denominator from Globex.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how fractions work.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Hey, you want a discount if you buy both? Ha-ha!

jokes.text: Well, la-di-da, Lady 'Cheaterly.'
jokes.setup: Lisa rejecting Nelson's cheating services
jokes.punchline: Literary reference pun combining 'Lady Chatterley' with cheating theme
jokes.explanation: Sophisticated literary pun from school bully - unexpected cultural reference combined with perfect nickname

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha! You're turning down my cheating services? That's like rejecting 'Lady Chatterley's Lover' — everybody wants a little infidelity!

jokes.text: To be honest, I'm surprised and saddened- N-No. Not 'saddened.' What's the word? - Ah, yes. 'Delighted.'
jokes.setup: Skinner appearing to criticize Lisa's performance
jokes.punchline: Revealing complete opposite reaction while searching for vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Skinner's administrative doubletalk and vocabulary struggles during emotional reversal

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Lisa, your performance was... uh... let me find the right word here...

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: It was absolutely... *searches* ...magnificent! Yes, magnificent!

jokes.text: Well, then, one could make the argument... that there really is no problem
jokes.setup: Lisa confessing to cheating
jokes.punchline: Skinner suggesting cover-up with bureaucratic language
jokes.explanation: Skinner's moral flexibility when money is involved - administrative corruption disguised as rationalization

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I cheated on the test, Principal Skinner. I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, Simpson, I'm afraid this is a serious matter. However, if we file the proper forms in triplicate, we can classify this as a 'learning experience anomaly' rather than academic dishonesty. The state will never know the difference.

jokes.text: Now, now. Leave the money out of this. It's not the money's fault you cheated
jokes.setup: Lisa saying grant money is tainted by cheating
jokes.punchline: Skinner personifying money as innocent victim of circumstances
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic treating money as separate entity with its own moral standing

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Money isn't tainted, Lisa. It's just a victim of circumstance, like all of us.

jokes.text: [Loud Explosion] Skinner! I am outraged that you've kept this from me. You were supposed to call as soon as the new scoreboard was in.
jokes.setup: Chalmers arriving dramatically during moral crisis
jokes.punchline: His outrage is about missing scoreboard demonstration, not ethical issues
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - building dramatic tension for completely mundane concern

dialogue.speaker: Superintendent Chalmers
dialogue.line: I have arrived to address this moral crisis at once!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Superintendent, thank goodness you're here. We have a serious situation—

dialogue.speaker: Superintendent Chalmers
dialogue.line: I don't want to hear excuses, Seymour! I'm here about the missing scoreboard demonstration! Do you have any idea how humiliating it is to reschedule my entire afternoon?

jokes.text: This school was once classified... the most dilapidated in all of Missouri. That's why it was shut down and moved here, brick by brick
jokes.setup: Chalmers explaining school's poor condition
jokes.punchline: Revelation that they literally moved worst school in Missouri to Springfield
jokes.explanation: Absurd bureaucratic solution - physically relocating failure instead of fixing it

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: The school's condition has deteriorated significantly. Crumbling walls, failing infrastructure, budget shortfalls...

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Superintendent, we're doing our best with limited resources.

dialogue.speaker: Chalmers
dialogue.line: Your best? Skinner, I have a confession to make. The state didn't build this school. We literally moved the worst school in Missouri here to save money.

jokes.text: Who can tell me the atomic weight of 'balonium'? / 'Dee-licious'? / Correct. I would also accept 'snack-tacular.'
jokes.setup: Chemistry class using promotional periodic table
jokes.punchline: Teaching fake science with marketing slogans as correct answers
jokes.explanation: Educational system corruption through corporate sponsorship creating fake science

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Class, today we're learning the periodic table of elements.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: But Mr. Teacher, this periodic table has Coca-Cola sponsorships all over it!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the element of surprise is brought to you by McDonald's. Now, what is the atomic number of hydrogen?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Um... Obey Your Thirst?

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Correct! And what about helium?

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: I'm Lovin' It?

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Excellent work. You're getting straight A's and also a free small fries.

jokes.text: Hi, Lisa. Hi, Super Nintendo Chalmers. / I'm 'learnding.'
jokes.setup: Ralph using computer for first time
jokes.punchline: Mispronunciation of 'learning' while being proud of computer use
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - mispronouncing word while demonstrating the concept, plus nickname for Chalmers

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm learnding!

jokes.text: How many can I put you down for? A lot? Please say a lot. I need this. / The wolf's at old Gil's door
jokes.setup: Gil trying to sell computers to school
jokes.punchline: Desperate sales pitch including personal financial crisis
jokes.explanation: Gil's pathetic sales approach breaking professional boundary with personal desperation

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: Well, Principal Seymour, these computers are top of the line. State of the art. And the price? Well, let's just say it's so low, I'm practically giving them away. In fact, I am giving them away because I haven't made a sale in three months and my wife left me and I'm living in my car!

jokes.text: These Colecos will rust up on you like that. Shut up, Gil. Close the deal. Close the deal
jokes.setup: Gil successfully making sale
jokes.punchline: Self-sabotaging with unnecessary add-on then coaching himself
jokes.explanation: Gil's self-defeating sales technique - adding problems then talking to himself

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: Well, that'll be $49.99 for the whole package.

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: But wait! How about I throw in our premium extended warranty for just $200 more?

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: No, no, no, Gil. What are you doing? You almost had 'em!

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: You're right. Sorry about that. The sale's yours.

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: No, wait—one more thing. What if we bundle in our deluxe installation service?

dialogue.speaker: Gil
dialogue.line: Oh, geez. Gil, you magnificent idiot.

jokes.text: We stress tough love- daily chores and the like. / No. We're not sending the lobster away to some snobby boarding school
jokes.setup: Sea Captain offering lobster training academy
jokes.punchline: Homer treating lobster education like privileged child schooling
jokes.explanation: Absurd parenting concerns applied to crustacean - Homer's class consciousness about lobster education

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Welcome to me Lobster Training Academy! We'll teach your crustacean everything it needs to know.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Does it have a tutor? My lobster needs SAT prep and a legacy connection to an Ivy League school.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: It's a lobster, lad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then I want my money back. I didn't pay good money for a mediocre education.

jokes.text: Who among you can honestly say you've never cheated? On your wives or your husbands
jokes.setup: Defending Lisa's academic cheating
jokes.punchline: Shifting to marital infidelity as universal experience
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection from academic to marital cheating - unexpected moral equivalency

dialogue.speaker: Comptroller Atkins
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure we can work something out. After all, we're all human.

dialogue.speaker: Comptroller Atkins
dialogue.line: My wife doesn't understand me either.

jokes.text: And where I come from- Canada- we reward courage.
jokes.setup: Comptroller praising Lisa's honesty
jokes.punchline: Explaining moral stance by being Canadian
jokes.explanation: Mild Canadian stereotype joke explaining moral behavior through nationality

dialogue.speaker: State Comptroller
dialogue.line: Your honesty is refreshing, young lady. In my experience, most people will lie for even the smallest personal gain.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I was raised to always tell the truth.

dialogue.speaker: State Comptroller
dialogue.line: That's admirable. You know, I'd be the same way, but I'm Canadian.

jokes.text: I'll be in my trailer. / Oh, good old predictable Lisa. I knew her conscience was a ticking time bomb
jokes.setup: Revelation that comptroller was fake
jokes.punchline: Skinner's elaborate deception plan based on Lisa's predictable morality
jokes.explanation: Meta revelation of elaborate con job based on character psychology - Skinner understanding Lisa better than audience

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: You see, I knew Lisa would eventually discover the truth about our fake comptroller. So I devised an elaborate plan based on her predictable moral code.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Principal Skinner, how could you?

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: It was simple, really. I knew you'd feel obligated to expose the fraud for the greater good of the school. So I made sure that when you did, you'd inadvertently solve an even bigger problem I'd created.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's... actually kind of genius.

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: I know. I've been reading books on child psychology.

jokes.text: Oh, good old predictable Lisa. I knew her conscience was a ticking time bomb.
jokes.setup: Lisa confessed as expected
jokes.punchline: Skinner treating Lisa's integrity as a dangerous flaw
jokes.explanation: Excellent character insight - Skinner's cynical view of Lisa's moral consistency as predictable problem

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Your honesty is a serious problem, Miss Simpson. In my experience, integrity is a threat to order and discipline.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Principal Skinner, isn't honesty something to be encouraged?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Not when it disrupts my carefully maintained system of authoritarian control. I'm going to have to ask you to be less truthful in the future.

jokes.text: I know a liquor store where we can cash this right now
jokes.setup: Fake comptroller finishing his performance
jokes.punchline: Suggesting illegal check cashing at inappropriate business
jokes.explanation: Actor's criminal suggestion for disposing of prop - continuing deception theme

dialogue.speaker: Fake Comptroller
dialogue.line: Thank you, thank you! You've been a wonderful audience!

dialogue.speaker: Fake Comptroller
dialogue.line: And remember, if you need to cash a check with no questions asked, just come see me at the abandoned warehouse by the docks!

jokes.text: Sorry, Marge. Pinchy got all dirty in the yard chasing birds. But don't worry. I put him in a nice hot bath
jokes.setup: Homer explaining Pinchy's whereabouts casually
jokes.punchline: Accidentally cooking lobster while trying to clean it
jokes.explanation: Homer's innocent care accidentally becoming cooking - tragic irony of good intentions

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just trying to clean Pinchy in some hot water, and I guess I wasn't paying attention...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So I kind of... cooked him.

jokes.text: Sorry, Marge. Pinchy got all dirty in the yard chasing birds. But don't worry. I put him in a nice hot bath.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining Pinchy's muddy claw prints
jokes.punchline: Homer accidentally cooking Pinchy while trying to clean him
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy setup - Homer's innocent explanation revealing tragic accident

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll just give Pinchy a nice bath to get those muddy claw prints off.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh, the water's nice and hot... perfect for cleaning a lobster.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... why isn't Pinchy moving?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no! I cooked him!

jokes.text: Oh, man, that's good. Oh, pass the butter. [Sobbing] Are you gonna eat that all by yourself? Uh-huh. Pinchy would've wanted it this way
jokes.setup: Homer mourning Pinchy's death
jokes.punchline: Eating the mourned pet while crying and justifying it
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy - genuine grief combined with gluttony and self-justification. Homer at his most contradictory.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, Pinchy... *sobs* I can't believe you're gone...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *takes a bite* Mmm... delicious. Well, Pinchy would've wanted it this way.

jokes.text: Oh, God! That's tasty. I wish Pinchy were here to enjoy this. [Sobbing]
jokes.setup: Homer continuing to eat Pinchy
jokes.punchline: Homer wishing Pinchy could taste himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark irony - Homer's innocent wish for Pinchy to enjoy his own flavor while Homer devours him

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, Pinchy, I wish you could taste yourself!

tmdb_overview: Stuck at home with a cold, Lisa gets addicted to a video game and neglects her homework. Her A+++ on a test wins the school a grant, but when she confesses that she cheated, Skinner covers it up. Meanwhile, Homer raises a lobster.

guest_stars.name: Maggie Roswell
guest_stars.character: Gavin's Mom / Miss Hoover / Helen Lovejoy (voice)

insight: Lisa's moral compromise drives the episode's darkest laughs despite middling overall density.
